name: Eastern Galician

ancestors.notes: Asturleonese/Leonese adstrate: stronger in eastern Lugo and eastern Ourense, where Galician and Asturleonese were historically in closer contact. Features such as diphthongised vowels (ie/ue in stressed position from Latin Ĕ/Ŏ) and stronger /tʃ/ for ⟨ch⟩ reflect Asturian influence. The Galician-Asturian transitional zone (A Fala, Eonaviego) shows heavy mixing.
Celtic substrate of the interior highlands.
Lusitanian substrate traces in the eastern border zone.

notes: Eastern Galician (galego oriental), spoken in the eastern provinces of Lugo and Ourense, bordering Asturias and Castile-León. KEY FEATURES: (1) STRONGER ASTURLEONESE CONTACT: the eastern zone was historically the zone of maximum Galician-Asturleonese overlap. The transitional belt 'Eonaviego' or 'A Fala' spoken in the Eo-Navia region (western Asturias) is a direct intermediate. (2) DIPHTHONG PRESERVATION/INNOVATION: stressed short Latin vowels Ĕ/Ŏ may show diphthongised realisations (/je/, /we/) in some lexical items under Asturleonese influence, which standard Western Galician monophthongised. (3) ⟨ch⟩ = /tʃ/ RETAINED more consistently (vs. western and urban Galician where /ʃ/ is spreading). (4) /ʎ/ for ⟨ll⟩ robustly preserved (no yeísmo, influenced by Asturian conservatism). (5) No gheada (characteristic of the western coast, absent in the east). (6) Some archaic Galaico-Portuguese features retained longer due to geographic isolation. Sources: Fernández Rei (1990), García García (1983), Beswick (2007) *Regional Nationalism in Spain*.
